events.text: Patient is a 46-year-old female of Hispanic/Latina ethnicity (mestizo), non-smoker, BMI 27.3, with no significant past medical history and a family history of gastric cancer in a paternal uncle.
At age 45, patient presented with rectal bleeding, mucus in stool, and a 10-pound weight loss over 2 months; colonoscopy identified a circumferential mass in the rectosigmoid junction (C19.9).
Rectosigmoid biopsy showed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma (histology 8140/3) with prominent Crohn-like lymphoid reaction and no mucinous component.

Next-generation sequencing revealed KRAS G12D mutation, BRAF wild-type, no PIK3CA mutation, APC loss-of-function mutation; MSI-high by PCR-based testing with MLH1 promoter hypermethylation and loss of MLH1/PMS2 by IHC; tumor mutational burden 38 mutations/Mb.

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at age 45 demonstrated rectosigmoid mass with bilateral pelvic sidewall lymphadenopathy and three hepatic metastases in the right lobe (largest 3.1 cm in segment V); no pulmonary metastases; staged T3N2bM1a.
At age 45, baseline CEA was 64 ng/mL and CA 19-9 was 47 U/mL; despite KRAS G12D mutation, MSI-high status makes patient a candidate for first-line immunotherapy per KEYNOTE-177.
At age 45, patient started first-line pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks for MSI-high metastatic colorectal cancer.

Restaging CT after 4 cycles of pembrolizumab showed mixed response with initial increase in hepatic lesion size (pseudoprogression suspected) but decrease in pelvic lymphadenopathy; CEA decreased to 38 ng/mL despite imaging findings.
At age 46, tumor board reviewed imaging and declining CEA; consensus was probable pseudoprogression; recommended continued pembrolizumab with repeat imaging in 8 weeks.
CT at age 46 after 8 cycles of pembrolizumab confirmed partial response with all hepatic lesions decreased (largest now 1.6 cm) and resolved pelvic lymphadenopathy; CEA 9.2 ng/mL.
At age 46, patient developed grade 2 immune-related colitis with 4-6 watery stools daily and cramping; managed with oral budesonide 9 mg daily without need for pembrolizumab interruption.
At age 46, patient experienced grade 1 arthralgia in bilateral knees and wrists attributed to immune-related adverse effect; managed with naproxen.
At age 46, CA 19-9 normalized to 14 U/mL; colitis symptoms improving on budesonide taper; patient continues pembrolizumab with excellent ECOG 0 performance status.

PET/CT at age 46 after 12 cycles showed near-complete metabolic response with minimal residual FDG uptake in segment V hepatic lesion (SUVmax 2.1, previously 9.8); no FDG-avid disease at primary site.

At age 46, multidisciplinary discussion about pursuing local therapy to residual hepatic lesion versus continued pembrolizumab alone; patient preferred non-surgical approach; plan to continue pembrolizumab.
At age 46, patient underwent elective laparoscopic low anterior resection of rectosigmoid primary to address ongoing intermittent bleeding and tenesmus from residual primary tumor.

Rectosigmoid resection specimen showed marked pathologic response with only microscopic foci of residual adenocarcinoma (less than 5% viable tumor), ypT1N0 (0 of 14 lymph nodes positive), prominent immune infiltrate consistent with immunotherapy effect.
At age 46, resumed pembrolizumab 4 weeks postoperatively; plan to complete 2 years total duration.
At age 46, post-surgical CEA was 2.8 ng/mL; hepatic lesion remains stable and likely represents treated disease; surveillance imaging every 3 months.
Surveillance CT at age 46 (3 months post-surgery) showed no new disease; residual hepatic segment V lesion unchanged at 1.4 cm and likely represents post-treatment scar; CEA 2.3 ng/mL.

scenario_blurb: Stage IV colorectal cancer with KRAS G12D mutation. Left-sided and right-sided primary tumors. Treated with FOLFOX or FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab. Some with liver-limited metastases considered for hepatic resection. MSI/MMR testing performed.
